card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, skin, and soft-tissue infections; selected mycobacterial infections; and eradication regimens for Helicobacter pylori in combination with other drugs.
card.mechanism: Binds to the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal at higher concentrations against susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Adults commonly receive 250 to 500 mg every 12 hours for 7 to 14 days depending on infection. Extended-release products are generally dosed once daily. H. pylori and mycobacterial regimens use combination therapy. Reduce dose in significant renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Immediate-release tablets and suspension may be taken with or without food. Extended-release tablets should be taken with food and swallowed whole. Shake suspension well; many suspensions are stored at room temperature and should not be refrigerated.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to macrolides; previous clarithromycin-associated cholestatic jaundice or hepatic dysfunction; and certain dangerous drug combinations, including ergot alkaloids, cisapride, pimozide, lomitapide, and lovastatin or simvastatin. Specific interaction contraindications vary by current label.
card.warnings: Can cause QT prolongation and torsades de pointes, severe hepatotoxicity, C. difficile diarrhea, serious hypersensitivity, and worsening myasthenia gravis. It is a strong CYP3A inhibitor with many potentially fatal interactions. Use caution in patients with coronary artery disease.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain, vomiting, headache, and altered or metallic taste.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, sudden cardiac death, severe liver injury, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, C. difficile colitis, pancreatitis, and severe drug-interaction toxicity.
card.interactions: Strongly inhibits CYP3A4 and can raise concentrations of many drugs, including colchicine, some calcium-channel blockers, benzodiazepines, immunosuppressants, and statins. Warfarin effect may increase. Other QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor infection response, cultures when available, renal and liver function, bowel pattern, ECG and electrolytes in high-risk patients, INR with warfarin, and concentrations or toxicity of interacting drugs.
card.patientTeaching: Complete the course and report jaundice, dark urine, fainting, palpitations, severe rash, or persistent watery diarrhea. Review every prescription, OTC drug, and supplement with a pharmacist before starting clarithromycin.
card.nutrition: Immediate-release products may be taken with food if nausea occurs; extended-release tablets should be taken with food. Maintain hydration. Avoid grapefruit if advised because interaction risk may be increased with some co-medications.

dosageForms: Topical solution, gel, lotion, or foam

card.indications: Treatment of serious susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive infections, including skin, bone, joint, intra-abdominal, pelvic, dental, and respiratory infections. Topical and vaginal products are used for acne or bacterial vaginosis depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Binds to the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal against selected organisms at higher concentrations.
card.dosing: Adult oral dosing commonly ranges from 150 to 450 mg every 6 hours depending on severity. IV dosing commonly totals 600 to 2700 mg daily in divided doses, with higher doses for life-threatening infection. Pediatric, topical, and vaginal dosing is product specific.
card.routeAdministration: Take oral capsules with a full glass of water and remain upright briefly to reduce esophageal irritation. IV clindamycin must be diluted and infused at the recommended rate; do not give undiluted IV push. Use topical or vaginal products exactly as directed.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clindamycin or lincomycin. Use caution with a history of colitis, severe GI disease, hepatic impairment, neuromuscular disorders, or previous C. difficile infection.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: clindamycin can cause severe and potentially fatal C. difficile-associated diarrhea and colitis, which may begin during therapy or weeks later. Other risks include severe hypersensitivity, severe skin reactions, hepatotoxicity, neutropenia, and neuromuscular blockade.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, vomiting, rash, itching, unpleasant taste, and local irritation with topical or vaginal use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: C. difficile colitis, anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, severe liver injury, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and profound neuromuscular weakness.
card.interactions: May enhance neuromuscular-blocking agents. Erythromycin can antagonize clindamycin activity and is generally not combined. Kaolin-pectin can reduce oral absorption. Vaginal products may weaken latex condoms or diaphragms for a product-specific period.
card.monitoring: Monitor infection response, bowel pattern during and after therapy, hydration, CBC and liver and renal function during prolonged treatment, and signs of hypersensitivity or superinfection.
card.patientTeaching: Complete therapy. Report watery or bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal cramps, fever, rash, facial swelling, or jaundice immediately. Do not self-treat severe diarrhea with antidiarrheals. Follow product directions regarding sexual activity and barrier contraception with vaginal formulations.
card.nutrition: Take oral capsules with a full glass of water; food may reduce stomach upset. Maintain hydration if diarrhea occurs. Probiotics should be discussed with the clinician, especially in immunocompromised patients.
